title: Introduction
text: Some algorithms need specific projection configurations to work correctly—or at all. In this lesson, you'll learn how relationship direction and weights affect algorithm behavior, and how to configure projections accordingly. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s148-000-new] As you can see here, the diagram illustrates the impact of relationship direction and weights on algorithm behavior, showing different configurations and how these factors influence the outcomes of graph algorithms.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to create undirected projections for algorithms that require them, include relationship weights to represent connection strength, project node properties and handle missing values with `coalesce()`, and determine what configuration an algorithm needs by checking its documentation.

title: Two Key Projection Settings
text: Beyond choosing which nodes and relationships to include, two configuration options significantly affect algorithms: relationship direction—directed vs undirected—and relationship weights, which treat some connections as stronger than others. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s150-002-new] Notice how this flowchart illustrates the decision-making process for selecting relationship direction and weights in graph projections, showing how these choices affect the execution of algorithms.

title: Directed vs Undirected
text: By default, GDS projections are directed—relationships flow one way. Some algorithms require undirected relationships, where connections work both ways. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s151-003-new] This illustration compares directed and undirected relationships, highlighting the differences in how connections are represented in each type.

title: When Direction Matters
text: Directed makes sense when direction has real meaning, like follows, purchased, or influenced, and when you want to distinguish incoming from outgoing connections. Undirected makes sense when relationships are naturally bidirectional, like collaborated with or friends with, or when the algorithm requires it, such as Leiden.

title: Algorithm Requirements
text: Check the documentation header for each algorithm. For example, Leiden's attributes show it requires undirected relationships. A green check means it works well, grey means it runs but ignores that aspect, and red means it won't run. Leiden has a red cross for directed—it requires undirected relationships.

title: Creating Undirected Projections
text: To create undirected projections, add `undirectedRelationshipTypes` to your projection configuration. Here's an example: MATCH (source:Actor)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Movie)<-[:ACTED_IN]-(target:Actor) WITH gds.graph.project('actor-network-undirected', source, target, {}, { undirectedRelationshipTypes: ['*'] }) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount. The `['*']` means all relationship types become undirected.

title: Comparing Directed vs Undirected
text: For symmetrical graphs, like actor collaborations, results may be identical. If A to B exists, B to A also exists, so making it undirected doesn't change the structure. For asymmetrical graphs, like followers, results will differ significantly. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s155-006-new] This diagram compares the effects of directed vs undirected projections on symmetrical and asymmetrical graphs, showing how these projections influence graph analysis.

title: Degree Centrality and Direction
text: Degree centrality counts outgoing relationships by default. You can change this with 'NATURAL' for outgoing, 'REVERSE' for incoming, and 'UNDIRECTED' for both directions. Here's an example: CALL gds.degree.stream('actor-network', { orientation: 'NATURAL' }).

title: Direction on Bipartite Graphs
text: Remember the Actor to Movie bipartite projection? With REVERSE, you're now counting incoming relationships, which means you're ranking Movies by how many actors appeared in them, not actors by how many movies they made. Direction determines what you're measuring. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s157-008-new] This illustration of a bipartite graph shows the direction of relationships and how reversing them changes the focus of analysis.

title: Relationship Weights
text: Not all connections are equal. Weights let algorithms treat some relationships as stronger than others. Examples include rating scores, transaction amounts, interaction frequency, and distance or travel time. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s158-009-new] This chart illustrates examples of relationship weights, such as rating scores and transaction amounts, and their impact on graph analysis.

title: Projecting Weights
text: Include relationship properties in your projection. For example: MATCH (source:User)-[r:RATED]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project('user-movie-weighted', source, target, { relationshipProperties: r { .rating } }, { undirectedRelationshipTypes: ['*'] }) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount. The `r { .rating }` syntax captures the `rating` property from relationships.

title: Using Weights in Algorithms
text: Tell the algorithm which property to use. For example: CALL gds.leiden.stream('user-movie-weighted', { relationshipWeightProperty: 'rating' }) YIELD nodeId, communityId. Without this parameter, the algorithm ignores the weights even if they're in the projection.

title: Weighted vs Unweighted Communities
text: Unweighted groups users who rated the same movies regardless of how they rated them. Weighted groups users who rated movies similarly, with 5-star ratings contributing more than 1-star. Weights transform "what's connected" into "how strongly it's connected." [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s161-012-new] This diagram compares unweighted and weighted community detection, illustrating how weights influence group formation.

title: Weights for Different Algorithms
text: Different algorithms interpret weights differently. Community Detection uses stronger connections to keep nodes together, Pathfinding treats weights as distances or costs to minimize, and Similarity uses higher weights to increase similarity contribution. Check documentation for how each algorithm uses weights.

title: Node Properties
text: Sometimes algorithms need node attributes, not just connections. Examples include initial values for community detection, features for similarity calculations, and attributes for machine learning pipelines. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s163-014-new] This illustration shows examples of node attributes and their uses in algorithms, such as initial values and features for similarity.

title: Projecting Node Properties
text: Include node properties using `sourceNodeProperties` and `targetNodeProperties`. For example: MATCH (source:Movie)<-[r:RATED]-(:User)-[:RATED]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project('user-movie-with-properties', source, target, { sourceNodeProperties: source { .startYear, .imdbRating }, targetNodeProperties: target { .startYear, .imdbRating } }) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount.

title: Handling Missing Properties
text: Use `coalesce()` to provide default values when properties might be missing. For example: MATCH (source:Movie)<-[:RATED]-(:User)-[:RATED]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project('movie-network-defaults', source, target, { sourceNodeProperties: source { imdbRating: coalesce(source.imdbRating, 5.0), startYear: coalesce(source.startYear, 1) }, targetNodeProperties: target { imdbRating: coalesce(target.imdbRating, 5.0), startYear: coalesce(target.startYear, 1) } }) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount.

title: No defaults leads to null
text: Without `coalesce()`, nodes missing the property will have `null` values, which can cause algorithms to fail. For example: CALL gds.fastRP.stream('user-movie-with-properties', { featureProperties: ['startYear', 'imdbRating'], embeddingDimension: 64 }) YIELD nodeId, embedding. Without defaults, this query will fail with a null property error. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s166-017-new] This illustration shows the impact of missing node properties on algorithm execution, focusing on the consequences of null values.

title: Using Node Properties in Algorithms
text: With the use of defaults, however, we can still run the algorithm. For example: CALL gds.fastRP.stream('movie-network-defaults', { featureProperties: ['startYear', 'imdbRating'], embeddingDimension: 64 }) YIELD nodeId, embedding. The `featureProperties` parameter tells FastRP to incorporate those node attributes into the embeddings. We'll cover FastRP along with many other algorithms later in the workshop.

title: Configuration Checklist
text: Before running an algorithm, ask yourself: Does this algorithm support my graph structure? Does it need undirected relationships, like Leiden does? Should I use weights, and do connection strengths matter? Do I need node properties, and does the algorithm use node attributes? What direction makes sense, and what am I actually measuring?

title: Quick Reference: Configuration
text: Here's a quick reference for configuration settings: For undirected relationships, use `undirectedRelationshipTypes: ['*']` in the second config map. For relationship weights, use `relationshipProperties: r { .propertyName }` in the first config map. For node properties, use `sourceNodeProperties: source { .prop }` and `targetNodeProperties: target { .prop }` in the first config map. To use relationship weights, specify `relationshipWeightProperty: 'propertyName'` in the algorithm config. To use node properties, specify `featureProperties: ['prop1', 'prop2']` in the algorithm config.

title: Summary
text: Projection configuration affects what algorithms can run and how they behave. Direction is crucial because some algorithms require undirected relationships, like Leiden, and direction determines what you're measuring, such as outgoing vs incoming. Use `undirectedRelationshipTypes: ['*']` to make relationships bidirectional. Weights capture connection strength with `relationshipProperties` and tell algorithms to use weights with `relationshipWeightProperty`, transforming analysis from "connected" to "how strongly connected." Node properties include attributes with `sourceNodeProperties` and `targetNodeProperties`, and use `coalesce()` to handle missing values. Some algorithms use properties as features, like FastRP with `featureProperties`. You're now ready to apply these concepts in the hands-on use case exercises. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-8-projection-configuration-s170-021-new] This diagram summarizes the effects of direction, weights, and node properties on algorithm behavior, with examples for each aspect.
